pause.text: After the meeting, we reviewed the report, and then we left.
If you have time, please call me, or send an email.
Well, to be honest, I think we should wait.
First, check your email, then join the call.
Yes, I understand, but we need more time.
Actually, now that I think about it, that makes sense.
When you're ready, let me know, and we'll begin.
Honestly, I'm not sure, but I'll find out.
Before you leave, please submit the form, and lock the door.
Overall, the project went well, despite a few delays.
By the way, I finished the report, so we're on track.
To be fair, everyone made mistakes, not just you.
In the end, we chose the simpler option, and it worked well.
As you know, the client called, and they had questions.
Looking back, I should have asked, but I didn't.
Once you're done, save the file, and send it over.
Naturally, we were nervous, but things went fine.
In short, the meeting helped, and we're moving forward.
Given the circumstances, we did our best, and that's enough.
Frankly, I was surprised, but pleasantly so.
Of course, I'll take care of it, right away.
Sure, that works for me, just let me know.
Believe it or not, I finished early, for once.

In fact, we already discussed this, last week.
Somehow, everything worked out, in the end.
Apparently, the flight got delayed, again.
Interestingly, nobody noticed, until much later.
Eventually, we found a solution, together.
Surprisingly, the test was easy, this time.
Meanwhile, back at the office, things were quiet.
Clearly, something went wrong, during the setup.

Admittedly, I was wrong, about the schedule.

speed.text: Every morning, our team meets for a quick stand-up call. Each person shares what they finished yesterday, what they plan to do today, and whether they are facing any blockers. This short meeting helps everyone stay on the same page without needing long email threads. After the call, we usually spend a few minutes reviewing the board together. It keeps the whole team aligned and makes it much easier to catch problems early, before they turn into bigger issues later in the week.

Last weekend, I decided to explore a new part of the city that I had never visited before. I woke up early, packed a small bag, and took the first train out of downtown. The streets were quiet, the shops were just opening, and the smell of fresh coffee filled the air. I spent hours walking around, taking photos, and chatting with local shopkeepers. By the time I got home, I felt refreshed and genuinely grateful for such a simple, relaxing day.

Learning a new language takes patience, consistency, and a genuine willingness to make mistakes along the way. Many people give up too early because they expect to sound perfect within just a few weeks. In reality, real progress happens slowly, through daily practice, small conversations, and constant repetition. The learners who improve the fastest are usually the ones who keep speaking, even when they feel unsure, rather than waiting until they feel completely ready.

Smartphones have completely changed the way we communicate with each other. A decade ago, sending a quick message meant waiting until you got home to a computer, but now everything happens instantly, no matter where you are. This constant connection has real benefits, like staying in touch with family across the world, but it also comes with a cost. Many people find it harder to focus, disconnect, or simply enjoy a quiet moment without checking their phone every few minutes.

Eating a balanced diet is one of the simplest ways to improve your overall health, yet it's often the hardest habit to maintain. Between busy schedules and constant temptations, it's easy to reach for something quick instead of something nutritious. Small changes usually work better than drastic ones - adding more vegetables, drinking more water, or simply cooking at home a few extra times a week. Over time, these little adjustments add up to a noticeably healthier lifestyle.

Preparing for a job interview can feel overwhelming, especially if it's been a while since your last one. The key is to research the company thoroughly, practice answering common questions out loud, and prepare a few thoughtful questions of your own to ask at the end. Confidence matters just as much as your answers, so try to relax, breathe, and remind yourself that the interviewer wants you to succeed just as much as you do. Preparation is what turns nervousness into genuine confidence.

Managing money well is a skill that takes practice, not just knowledge. Many people know they should save more, but putting that into action is a different story. A simple approach is to set aside a fixed amount every month before spending on anything else, rather than saving whatever happens to be left over. Building an emergency fund, even a small one, can also reduce a lot of daily stress. Over time, these habits make a real difference, even if the progress feels slow at first.

Small daily choices can add up to a real difference for the environment over time. Carrying a reusable bottle, walking instead of driving for short trips, and reducing food waste are all simple habits that don't require much effort. Of course, individual actions alone won't solve every problem, but they do build awareness and often inspire people around us to think differently too. Change usually starts small, spreads gradually, and eventually becomes part of how a community lives.

Maintaining close friendships as an adult is harder than most people expect it to be. Between work, family responsibilities, and simply being tired, it's easy to let months slip by without checking in on people who matter. The friendships that last are usually the ones where both people make a small, consistent effort - a quick message, a short call, or making time to meet even when schedules are busy. It rarely takes much, just consistency.

Public speaking is one of the most common fears people report, even more than the fear of failure itself. Yet almost everyone who becomes comfortable speaking in front of others started out nervous, with shaky hands and a racing heart. The difference is practice - rehearsing out loud, recording yourself, and gradually exposing yourself to slightly bigger audiences over time. Confidence rarely arrives before you start; it shows up gradually, built through repetition rather than talent.

missingSound.tip: Don't drop the ending - say all three sounds: /s/ /k/ /t/.
Keep the soft "th" sound before the z - it isn't the same word as "close".
Keep the "th" sound before the final s.
There are two r sounds - li-BRAR-y, not li-BA-ry.
A hard cluster - try to keep all four sounds: /s/ /ɪ/ /k/ /s/ /θ/ /s/.
Keep the r sound: wor-l-d, not "wold".
Keep the d sound before b: HAND-bag.
Keep both c sounds: ARC-tic, not "artic".
Keep the t sound: want-ED, not "wanned".
Keep the t before the s: STU-dents.
Keep the d and th sounds: wid-ths.
Keep the final t sound: is-n't.
Keep the final t sound: did-n't.
Keep the final t sound: would-n't.
Keep the final t sound: nex-t.
Keep the t before the s: fac-ts.
Keep the ng and th sounds: streng-ths.

minimalPairs.tip: "v" (very, top teeth on lip) vs "b" (berry, lips together).
"v" (top teeth on lip) vs "w" (rounded lips, no teeth).
"r" (tongue doesn't touch the roof of your mouth) vs "l" (tongue touches the roof of your mouth).
"l" (tongue touches the roof of your mouth) vs "r" (tongue doesn't touch).
"s" (tongue behind your teeth) vs "th" (tongue between your teeth).
"f" (top teeth on lip) vs "th" (tongue between your teeth).
"f" (voiceless, no vocal cords) vs "v" (voiced, vocal cords buzz) - same lip position.
"p" (voiceless) vs "b" (voiced) at the end of the word.
"w" (rounded lips, no teeth) vs "v" (top teeth on lip).
"cl" vs "gl" - voiceless vs voiced first sound.
"l" (tongue touches the roof of your mouth) vs "r" (tongue doesn't touch).

intonationEmotions.label: 😄 Happy
intonationEmotions.hint: Bright, bouncy pitch with a lot of up-and-down variation.

intonationEmotions.label: 😔 Sad
intonationEmotions.hint: Low, flat, and slow - not much pitch variation.

intonationEmotions.label: 😠 Angry
intonationEmotions.hint: Louder, sharper, with more force behind it.

intonationEmotions.label: 😲 Surprised
intonationEmotions.hint: Pitch rises sharply, especially toward the end.

You have got to be kidding me.
I don't know what to say right now.
This is exactly what I needed today.
Wow, I did not see that coming.
That is the last thing I expected.
Honestly, I wasn't ready for this.
I really need your help with this.
You have no idea what just happened.
I've never seen anything like it.
Please tell me you're joking right now.
I think we need to talk about this.
Nobody warned me about this at all.
